scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (UTG) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: K♠ 7♦ 2♣ is very dry: 7 hand-types in the UTG range flop strong value. Only top-pair-good-kicker and better connect. A textbook c-bet board — fire small, it gets through often.

scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (UTG) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: A♥ K♦ 4♣ is dry, ace-high: 6 hand-types in the UTG range flop strong value. Ace-high dry boards favor the preflop raiser hugely — they have all the AK, AQ, AA. The caller rarely has an ace.

scenario: Villain (MP, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (MP) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: J♥ 6♦ 2♣ is dry: 8 hand-types in the MP range flop strong value. A disconnected jack-high board. The raiser's overpairs and AJ/KJ dominate. Easy c-bet spot.

scenario: Villain (BTN, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (BTN) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: Q♦ T♦ 8♥ is wet: 12 hand-types in the BTN range flop strong value. Many more hands connect here — pairs, two pairs, and the board favors broadway-heavy ranges. Bet bigger for protection. J9s makes the Q-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q) and belongs in strong value.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (CO) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: 9♠ 8♠ 7♦ is very wet: 14 hand-types in the CO range flop strong value. The most connected board type. So many hands connect hard with this that a c-bet faces lots of resistance — check back medium hands. JTs makes the J-high straight (7-8-9-T-J).

scenario: Villain (BTN, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (BTN) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: K♣ 8♦ 2♣ is semi-wet (flush draw): 9 hand-types in the BTN range flop strong value. The two-tone king-high board: top pair is strong but the flush draw means betting for protection matters more than on K72 rainbow.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (CO) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: A♠ 9♦ 4♠ is semi-wet: 10 hand-types in the CO range flop strong value. Ace-high with a flush draw. Strong for the raiser's range but the spade draw means thin value hands should bet to deny equity.

scenario: Villain (BTN, TAG) opened. You're reading their range on this flop.
question: From Villain's (BTN) opening range, tap every hand that flopped TOP PAIR (good kicker) or better.
explanation: Q♥ J♦ T♣ is wet, connected: 15 hand-types in the BTN range flop strong value. Three broadways — tons of two pair, straights, and pair+draw combos connect. A dangerous board even though it's rainbow. Note: AKs and AKo both flop the nut straight (A-K-Q-J-T) and belong in the 'or better' category. K9s makes the K-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). 98s/98o make the Q-high straight (8-9-T-J-Q).

scenario: Villain (UTG, TAG) opens 3BB. You (BTN) call. Blinds fold.
question: Before the flop — select Villain's (UTG) entire opening range.
explanation: This is your starting point — the UTG range from your preflop trainer (66+, ATs+, KJs+, JTs, AJo+, KQo). Every narrowing step from here removes combos. You can't read a range you can't first reconstruct.

scenario: Flop: K♠ 7♦ 2♣ (dry, rainbow). The TAG continuation-bets 3/4 pot.
question: Villain (TAG) c-bets value hands and overcard semi-bluffs on this dry board, checking medium pairs. Select their c-betting range.
explanation: Value: AA, KK (sets/top set), AK, KQ (top pair). Semi-bluff: AQ/AJ/AT with overcard + backdoor equity. The TAG CHECKS QQ/JJ/TT/99/88/66 (medium pairs, pot control — they don't want to bloat the pot and get raised off the best hand) and gives up KJ/JT (whiffed). Note QQ-TT are now removed — they'd check here. That's the narrowing.

scenario: You call the flop c-bet. Turn: 3♥ (blank). The TAG fires a second barrel.
question: Your flop call says you have a king or a pair. The TAG now barrels only what beats your range or has strong equity. Select the double-barrel range.
explanation: When you call the flop, the TAG reads you for Kx or a pocket pair. The blank 3♥ changes nothing. A disciplined TAG now value-bets only AA, KK, 77 (sets) and AK (top pair top kicker). The AQ/AJ/AT semi-bluffs GIVE UP — you called once, you won't fold now, and they have no equity. KQ also often checks back here for pot control. Range shrank from 11 combo-types to 5. By the river you could name his exact holding.

scenario: Villain (CO, TAG) opens. The BB calls. (You're observing to practice reading.)
question: Select Villain's (CO) opening range (the wider steal-range).
explanation: CO opens ~22% — much wider than UTG. This includes all pairs, all suited aces, suited connectors down to 76s, and broadway offsuit hands. A wider starting range means more combos to narrow as the hand develops.

scenario: Flop: J♥ 9♥ 4♣ (two-tone, hearts). The BB checks, Villain c-bets, the BB CHECK-RAISES.
question: Villain (TAG) check-raises this two-tone board with strong made hands AND flush draws. Select the hand-types in their check-raise range. (Suit note in explanation.)
explanation: The BB check-raises three things: SETS (JJ, 99, 44), two pair (J9s), and FLUSH DRAWS. Crucial suit point — of the suited hands shown (KQs, QTs, T8s, AJs), only the HEART combos actually have the flush draw on this J♥9♥4♣ board. KhQh, QhTh, Th8h, AhJh can semi-bluff check-raise; the spade/club/diamond versions of those same hands cannot — they'd just call or fold. The grid marks the hand-types, but at the table you'd only raise the specific suit that connects. This is the core of approach B: the grid shows types, your reasoning supplies the suit.

scenario: Villain (BTN, TAG) opens (wide steal). You defend BB and call. Flop: 8♦ 5♣ 3♠ (low, dry).
question: You check, Villain c-bets small (1/3 pot) — a typical steal c-bet on a low board. This bets almost their whole range as a bluff-heavy c-bet. Select the hands a TAG keeps betting for VALUE here (not bluffs).
explanation: On a low dry board the BTN c-bets a huge portion of their range cheaply, but only some hands are VALUE: overpairs (AA-99), top pair (A8s, 98s, 87s, 86s — eights), and sets (88, 55, 33). Everything else — AK, AQ, KQ, suited broadways — is a bluff/give-up using backdoor equity. Recognizing the value portion tells you what beats you when they keep firing. Their range is wide now but narrows fast if they barrel turn and river.

scenario: You call the flop. Turn: K♥. The TAG barrels again (big sizing, 3/4 pot).
question: The K♥ is a scare card that hits the BTN's range hard. Villain (TAG) barrels value (now including new top pair) plus picks up some hearts as draws. Select the turn-barrel value range.
explanation: The K♥ turn is a classic barrel card — it favors the raiser's range (they have AK, KQ, KK; you rarely do as the BB caller). Value now: KK (set), AK/KQ (new top pair), AA/QQ/JJ (overpairs still good), sets (88, 55, 33). Note TT/99 and the weak top-pair eights (A8s, 98s, 87s) often CHECK now — they're no longer comfortable barreling for value with the king out there. A TAG also adds heart flush draws as bluffs. When this player barrels a scare card, respect it — their range is now king-heavy and strong.

scenario: YOUR HAND (3-bet pot). You open BTN with K♠T♠, a TAG 3-bets from the blinds, you call. Flop J♠Q♣6♦. Villain bets, you call. Turn (blank), villain bets again, you call. River 3♣ — villain fires a third barrel, near pot-sized.
hud: Villain: TAG · 3-bet range here = QQ+/AK for value at micro
question: Villain barrels all three streets on Q-J-6-x-x. What does their range hit, and what should you do with K-high (no pair)?
options: Call — they could be bluffing with AK
Fold — a TAG firing 3 barrels into this board has QQ+/sets/AK that all beat K-high; you have no pair and no draw got there
Raise as a bluff — represent the straight
Call — you have two overcards to the board
explanation: This is your actual hand (lost ~97 BB). Villain's 3-bet range was QQ+/AK. The board J-Q-6 connects hard with that range: AK now has a pair+gutshot or top pair, QQ is an overpair, JJ/sets are crushing you. A TAG does NOT fire three barrels as a bluff at micro. You held K-high with no pair — you were drawing nearly dead by the turn. The discipline: when a tight player's range beats your hand and they keep betting big, fold. You don't need to 'catch' a bluff that isn't there.

scenario: YOUR HAND. You raise BTN with A♣Q♣, a player 4-bets preflop, you call. Flop 8h 4c Ks. Checks through. Turn (blank) villain bets, you call. River 4d pairs the board — you bet, villain calls and shows Q♦Q♥.
hud: Villain: 4-bet preflop = extremely strong (QQ+/AK at micro)
question: Against a preflop 4-bet, you flopped ace-high on K-8-4. What was the core mistake?
options: Betting the river — value-betting ace-high into a 4-bettor's range that always beats it
Calling the turn was fine
Folding preflop would have been too tight
Nothing — A-high is a good bluff-catcher
explanation: Your real hand (lost ~105 BB). A 4-bet at micro is QQ+/AK — a range that has you crushed or dominated (AK has your AQ outkicked; QQ+ beats ace-high outright). You have ace-high on K-8-4: you beat nothing in their continuing range. Betting the river turns your hand into a bluff against a range that never folds the better hands and never called preflop with worse. Against a 4-bet, ace-high is a check-fold by the river, not a value bet. The leak: not respecting that the 4-bet range is uniformly strong.

scenario: YOUR HAND. Multiway limped pot, you over-limp T♠8♠ on the BTN. Flop A♠T♣3♥. Checks to you, you check back. Turn 8♣ gives you two pair-ish (tens and eights... actually pair of tens + pair... you have T8 = two pair tens and eights). You bet, one caller. River J♠. Villain bets big.
hud: Villain: unknown limper, called your turn bet, leads river
question: You have T8 (two pair: tens & eights) on A-T-3-8-J. Villain check-calls turn then leads river big. How does the river change things?
options: Call — two pair is strong, always good
Be cautious — J completes QT/KQ straights and the A-J two pair beats you; a limper leading big into you on the river often has it. Two pair (no top pair) is a bluff-catcher at best
Raise for value — two pair wants to get it in
Fold instantly — two pair is trash here
explanation: Based on your real T8s hand. Two pair feels strong, but locate it in villain's range: the A-T-3 flop means many of their calling hands contain an ace (Ax two pair now beats your tens-and-eights), and the J completes Q-T and K-Q straights. A passive limper who suddenly leads big into you on the river is rarely bluffing at micro. Your 'two pair' is actually a weak bluff-catcher because the better two pairs (Ax) and straights all got there. This is the over-limp downstream cost: you build a multiway pot with a hand that makes second-best hands.

scenario: You raise CO, the BB (a TAG) calls. Flop comes A♥K♦4♣.
question: Whose range does this A-K-4 flop favor?
options: BB's range — they have more aces and kings
Your range — as the preflop raiser you have far more AK, AQ, AA, KK; the BB's flatting range is capped (no AK/AA, those 3-bet)
Neither — it's a coin flip
BB's — they flopped a set more often
explanation: As the preflop raiser into a caller, A-K-4 hits YOUR range hard. You hold all the strong aces and kings (AK, AQ, AA, KK) that a tight BB would have 3-bet preflop, not flat-called. The BB's calling range is capped — mostly suited connectors, small pairs, suited broadways that miss this board. This is a board to c-bet aggressively: their range can't continue often.

scenario: You raise BTN, the BB calls. Flop 7♥6♥5♣.
question: Whose range does 7-6-5 (two-tone, connected) favor?
options: Your range — you have the overpairs
BB's range — their wide defending range (suited connectors, 89, 98, 44, sets, two pair, flush draws) connects with this board far more than your high-card-heavy opening range
Yours — you have AA/KK
Neither matters on a low board
explanation: This is a low, connected, wet board that favors the CALLER's range. The BB's defending range is full of hands that connect hard with 7-6-5: 98s, 89, 44-66, 87, 75s, plus flush draws. Your BTN opening range is high-card heavy (big aces, broadways) that mostly whiffs here. Even your overpairs (AA-88) are vulnerable on this draw-heavy texture. Lesson: c-bet less here, and don't stack off light — when the board favors their range, proceed cautiously.

scenario: You hold A♦Q♦. Two different boards: (X) K-Q-2 rainbow vs a tight 3-bettor who keeps betting. (Y) Q-7-2 rainbow vs a passive caller who check-calls once.
question: Same top pair (Q) — how does your relative hand strength differ between board X and Y?
options: Same strength — top pair is top pair
Strong on both
Weak on X (3-bettor's range = QQ+/AK all beat or dominate your pair of queens; the king also helps them), reasonable on Y (passive caller's range has many worse Qx, draws, that your AQ beats)
Weak on both — fold AQ always
explanation: Top pair is not one thing — its value is relative to villain's range. On board X (K-Q-2 vs a 3-bettor), their range is QQ+/AK: KK/QQ/AA beat you, AK has top pair better kicker or AA/KK, and AQ ties at best. Your pair of queens is crushed. On board Y (Q-7-2 vs a passive caller), their range has lots of worse queens (KQ, QJ, QT), draws, and middle pairs your AQ dominates. Identical card strength, opposite decision — because the range and board are different.

scenario: You raise, a NIT calls. Flop Q♠8♦3♣ (dry). You c-bet, nit calls. Turn 8♠ (pairs board). You bet again, nit raises.
question: A nit who called flop then RAISES the paired turn. You hold A-Q (top pair top kicker). Action?
options: Re-raise for value — TPTK is strong
Call and pay off — they could have a worse queen
Fold or proceed very cautiously — a nit raising a paired board represents trips (8x), an overpair, or a set; TPTK is now a bluff-catcher that beats almost nothing in a nit's raising range
Shove — never fold top pair top kicker
explanation: Player type sets the range, and a NIT's turn-raising range is the tightest in poker. On Q-8-3-8, a nit raises with trips (88, Q8, 83s), overpairs (99+), or a set — essentially nothing you beat. Your AQ was strong on the flop but the nit's RAISE redefines their range to hands that are well ahead of top pair. TPTK is a trophy hand for beginners but here it's a bluff-catcher against a range with no bluffs. Fold. This is the antidote to your biggest leak: when a tight player's aggression represents a range that beats one pair, believe them.

scenario: You raise BTN, BB (calling station) calls. Flop J♣9♦4♠. You c-bet, station calls. Turn 2♥. You hold A♠J♦ (top pair top kicker).
question: Against a CALLING STATION on J-9-4-2, you have TPTK. Best line?
options: Check — pot control against a station
Bet for value — a station calls with many worse Jx, 9x, draws, and gutshots; TPTK is ahead of their wide calling range and you want value
Fold — too dangerous
Check-fold the turn
explanation: Player type flips the answer. Against a calling STATION, TPTK on J-9-4-2 is a clear value bet — their range for calling is enormously wide (any Jx, 9x, gutshots, overcards, draws), and you beat almost all of it. The same hand you'd pot-control or fold against a nit, you bet thinly for value against a station. The station's weakness is calling too much, so you punish by value betting relentlessly with hands like TPTK. Read the player, then act.

scenario: You 3-bet preflop with A♥K♥, original raiser (TAG) calls. Flop 9♣6♠2♦ (dry, low).
question: You have AK-high (no pair) on 9-6-2. Whose range does the board favor, and what's your play?
options: Check — you missed

C-bet — as the 3-bettor, this dry low board favors YOUR range (overpairs AA-TT) far more than their flat-calling range; your AK has backdoors + overcards and a c-bet folds out their air. You represent the overpairs they can't have
Give up — AK is just ace-high
Check-call to bluff-catch
explanation: As the 3-bettor, your range is the strong one: AA, KK, QQ, AK — the overpairs dominate a 9-6-2 board. The caller's range (which would 4-bet AA/KK at micro) is capped at hands like TT-JJ, AQ, suited connectors. A c-bet leverages your range advantage: you credibly represent the overpairs they rarely hold, and even though you have only ace-high, you have two overcards and backdoor equity. This is range-based c-betting — the board favors your range, so you bet regardless of your specific holding.
